nodes.title: Identify a conic section as the intersection of a plane and a cone
nodes.brief: Recognise that a conic section is the curve produced when a plane cuts a double-napped right circular cone.
nodes.quote: If we take the intersection of a plane with a cone, the section so obtained is called a *conic section*. Thus, conic sections are the curves obtained by intersecting a right circular cone by a plane.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The double-napped right circular cone.
Take a fixed point V called the vertex, a line l through V called the axis, and a second line m through V making a constant angle alpha with l. Rotating m around l sweeps out a double-napped right circular cone: two mirror-image nappes meeting at V, extending infinitely in both directions.

PART 2 — A conic section is the intersection curve.
When a plane cuts this cone, the curve where the plane and cone meet is called a conic section. The shape of that curve depends entirely on how the cutting plane is oriented relative to the axis — not on any independent definition of circle, ellipse, parabola or hyperbola.

PART 3 — One family, four shapes.
Circles, ellipses, parabolas and hyperbolas are all members of the conic-section family. They are not unrelated shapes but different cross-sections of the same cone, unified by the single construction: rotate a generator about an axis and cut the resulting surface with a plane.
keyMoves: Picture the double-napped cone generated by rotating a generator about the axis at fixed angle alpha
Define a conic section as the curve where a plane meets the cone
Note vertex, axis, generator and the two nappes
misconceptions: Thinking the four conics are unrelated curves rather than slices of one cone
Forgetting the cone is double-napped (extends both ways from the vertex)

nodes.title: Classify the conic from the angle the cutting plane makes with the axis
nodes.brief: Decide whether the section is a circle, ellipse, parabola or hyperbola by comparing beta (plane-axis angle) with alpha (semi-vertical angle) and 90 degrees.
nodes.quote: When the plane cuts the nappe (other than the vertex) of the cone, we have the following situations:
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two angles that govern the section.
Let alpha be the semi-vertical angle of the cone (the constant angle the generator makes with the axis) and beta be the angle the cutting plane makes with the axis of the cone. These two angles together completely determine which conic you get.

PART 2 — Four non-degenerate cases by comparing beta with alpha and 90 degrees.
When the plane cuts a nappe without passing through the vertex: if beta = 90 degrees the section is a circle; if alpha < beta < 90 degrees it is an ellipse; if beta = alpha the plane is parallel to exactly one generator and the section is a parabola; if 0 <= beta < alpha the plane cuts both nappes and the section is a hyperbola.

PART 3 — Degenerate cases when the plane passes through the vertex.
If the cutting plane passes through the vertex V, the intersection shrinks to a degenerate conic: a single point (when the plane meets only the vertex), a single straight line (when the plane contains exactly one generator), or a pair of intersecting straight lines (when the plane cuts through both nappes at V). These are the limiting or degenerate forms of the four standard conics.
keyMoves: Compare beta with 90 degrees and with alpha
beta = 90 -> circle; alpha < beta < 90 -> ellipse; beta = alpha -> parabola; 0 <= beta < alpha -> hyperbola
Recognise vertex-cutting planes give degenerate conics (point, line, pair of lines)
misconceptions: Mixing up which inequality gives an ellipse versus a hyperbola
Forgetting that a hyperbola requires the plane to cut both nappes

nodes.title: Define a circle by its centre and radius
nodes.brief: State that a circle is the locus of points equidistant from a fixed centre, the common distance being the radius.
nodes.quote: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed point in the plane.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The locus condition.
A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at a fixed distance from a fixed point. The fixed point is called the centre of the circle, and the fixed distance is called the radius.

PART 2 — The defining condition in symbols.
If C is the centre and P is any point on the circle, then the defining condition is |CP| = r, where r is the radius. Every single point on the circle satisfies this equality, and every point not on the circle does not — that is precisely what a locus definition means.

PART 3 — Why this definition matters.
This single geometric condition |CP| = r is the starting point for deriving the algebraic equation of any circle. By expressing |CP| using the distance formula and squaring, we convert the geometric locus into an algebraic equation that can be manipulated and applied.
keyMoves: State the locus condition: every point is at the same distance from the centre
Name the fixed point the centre and the common distance the radius
Write the defining condition |CP| = r
misconceptions: Confusing the radius (centre-to-edge) with the diameter (edge-to-edge)
Thinking the centre lies on the circle

nodes.title: Write the equation of a circle with given centre and radius
nodes.brief: Apply the distance formula to the locus condition to get (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 for centre (h, k) and radius r.
nodes.quote: This is the required equation of the circle with centre at (h,k) and radius r.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Derive the equation from the locus condition.
Let the centre be C(h, k) and let P(x, y) be any point on the circle of radius r. The locus condition |CP| = r becomes, via the distance formula, sqrt((x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2) = r. Squaring both sides gives the standard equation of the circle: (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2.

PART 2 — Special case: centre at the origin.
When the centre is at the origin, h = 0 and k = 0, so the equation simplifies to x^2 + y^2 = r^2. This is the simplest form and occurs frequently in problems where no specific centre is given.

PART 3 — Apply: substitute given centre and radius directly.
To write the equation of a specific circle, substitute the given h, k, r into (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2. For example, centre (3, -2) and radius 5 gives (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25. Note that a negative k flips the sign in the bracket: y - (-2) = y + 2.
keyMoves: Apply the distance formula to |CP| = r
Square to get (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2
Substitute given centre (h, k) and radius r to write the specific equation
misconceptions: Writing (x + h)^2 + (y + k)^2 instead of subtracting the centre coordinates
Forgetting to square the radius on the right-hand side

refreshers.trigger: Student is asked to turn the locus condition |CP| = r into an algebraic equation but cannot recall or write the Class-10 distance formula for the distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2).
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we turn |CP| = r into an equation: if I give you two points on the grid, how do you find the straight-line distance between them?
ladder: Mark two points on graph paper, say (1, 2) and (4, 6), and join them with a straight line. Now draw a horizontal line from the first and a vertical line up to the second so you get a right-angled triangle. How long is the horizontal side, and how long is the vertical side?

The horizontal side is 4 - 1 = 3 (the difference in the x's) and the vertical side is 6 - 2 = 4 (the difference in the y's). The slanted line you actually want is the hypotenuse. Which old rule connects the two short sides of a right triangle to the hypotenuse?
Pythagoras: hypotenuse squared = (one side)^2 + (other side)^2. So here distance^2 = 3^2 + 4^2. What is the distance itself?
It is sqrt(3^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5. Now write that with letters instead of numbers: for any two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2), what goes inside the square root?
refreshers.answer: The distance between two points is just Pythagoras in disguise. Drop a horizontal and a vertical leg between the points and you get a right triangle: the horizontal leg has length (x2 - x1), the vertical leg has length (y2 - y1), and the distance you want is the hypotenuse. So the distance formula is sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2). The squaring means the signs do not matter - it works whichever point you call first. In this node that is exactly the tool that turns the geometry into algebra: the distance from the centre C(h, k) to a general point P(x, y) is sqrt((x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2), and setting that equal to the radius r gives sqrt((x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2) = r, which we then square to get (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2.
refreshers.returnCue: so with the distance from C(h, k) to P(x, y) written as sqrt((x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2), set it equal to r and square - that is the circle's equation.

nodes.title: Find a circle's centre and radius by completing the square
nodes.brief: Convert a general second-degree circle equation to standard form by completing the square, then read off the centre and radius.
nodes.quote: Now, completing the squares within the parenthesis, we get
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise the general form and group terms.
A circle often appears in expanded form x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0. To convert to standard form, group the x-terms together and the y-terms together: (x^2 + Dx) + (y^2 + Ey) = -F.

PART 2 — Complete the square in x and y.
For the x-group, add (D/2)^2 to both sides; for the y-group, add (E/2)^2 to both sides. This gives (x + D/2)^2 + (y + E/2)^2 = -F + (D/2)^2 + (E/2)^2. The right side must be positive for a real circle.

PART 3 — Read off centre and radius.
Compare (x + D/2)^2 + (y + E/2)^2 = R^2 with (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 to identify h = -D/2, k = -E/2, and r = sqrt(R^2). Example: x^2 + y^2 + 8x + 10y - 8 = 0 becomes (x + 4)^2 + (y + 5)^2 = 49, giving centre (-4, -5) and radius 7.
keyMoves: Group x-terms and y-terms together
Complete the square in x and in y, adding equal amounts to both sides
Compare with the standard form to read off centre (h, k) and radius r
misconceptions: Completing the square on the left without adding the same constants to the right
Reading the centre as (+4, +5) instead of (-4, -5) from (x + 4)^2 + (y + 5)^2

refreshers.trigger: Student cannot turn x^2 + Dx into (x + D/2)^2 - (D/2)^2, i.e. is unsure how to complete the square by adding (half the linear coefficient) squared, when converting x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 to standard circle form.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we group these terms: how do you turn something like x^2 + 8x into a perfect square like (x + something)^2?
ladder: You already know that (x + p)^2 multiplies out to x^2 + 2px + p^2. So if you saw x^2 + 8x and wanted it to look like the start of (x + p)^2, what would p have to be so that 2p equals 8?
Right, p = 4 - you take half of the 8. So (x + 4)^2 expands to x^2 + 8x + 16. Compare that with what you actually have, x^2 + 8x: what extra term did (x + 4)^2 sneak in that you never had?
It added a 16, which is exactly (8/2)^2. So to keep things equal, x^2 + 8x must equal (x + 4)^2 minus that 16. In general, what is x^2 + Dx equal to, written as a square minus a correction?
refreshers.answer: Completing the square turns x^2 + Dx into a perfect square plus a leftover. Since (x + D/2)^2 expands to x^2 + Dx + (D/2)^2, that square carries an extra (D/2)^2 you never had - so x^2 + Dx = (x + D/2)^2 - (D/2)^2. The recipe is always the same: take half of the coefficient of x, square it, add it to build the perfect square, and subtract it again (or add it to the other side of an equation) so nothing changes. In this node you do exactly this to both the x-group and the y-group: x^2 + Dx becomes (x + D/2)^2 after adding (D/2)^2, and y^2 + Ey becomes (y + E/2)^2 after adding (E/2)^2 - adding those same amounts to the right side too - which is how x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 collapses into (x + D/2)^2 + (y + E/2)^2 = R^2 and lets you read off the centre and radius.
refreshers.returnCue: Now that half-the-coefficient-squared is in hand, group the x-terms and y-terms and complete the square on each to get the standard form.

nodes.title: Define a parabola by its focus and directrix
nodes.brief: State that a parabola is the locus of points equidistant from a fixed focus and a fixed directrix line, and name its axis and vertex.
nodes.quote: A parabola is the set of all points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed line and a fixed point (not on the line) in the plane.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The two fixed elements: focus and directrix.
A parabola is the set of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed point F (called the focus) and a fixed straight line (called the directrix). The focus does not lie on the directrix.

PART 2 — The equidistance condition.
For any point P on the parabola, the distance from P to the focus equals the distance from P to the directrix: PF = PD, where D is the foot of the perpendicular from P to the directrix. This single condition defines which points belong to the parabola.

PART 3 — Axis and vertex.
The line through the focus perpendicular to the directrix is the axis of the parabola. The point where the axis meets the parabola is the vertex, and it is the midpoint between the focus and the directrix. At the vertex, PF = PD is minimised.
keyMoves: State the equidistance condition: distance to focus = distance to directrix
Name the fixed line the directrix and the fixed point the focus
Identify the axis (through focus, perpendicular to directrix) and the vertex
misconceptions: Letting the focus lie on the directrix (that gives a degenerate straight line)
Confusing the directrix (a line) with the axis (also a line, but through the focus)

nodes.title: Derive and use the standard equation y^2 = 4ax of a parabola
nodes.brief: Obtain y^2 = 4ax from the focus-directrix condition with vertex at the origin, and identify the four standard orientations.
nodes.quote: These four equations are known as standard equations of parabolas.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Derive y^2 = 4ax from the focus-directrix condition.
Place the vertex at the origin, focus at F(a, 0) with a > 0, and directrix x = -a. For any point P(x, y) on the parabola, PF = PD gives sqrt((x-a)^2 + y^2) = x + a (since x + a is the distance to the directrix). Squaring: (x-a)^2 + y^2 = (x+a)^2. Expanding: x^2 - 2ax + a^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2. Simplifying: y^2 = 4ax.

PART 2 — Identify orientation from the equation.
If the equation involves y^2, the axis of the parabola is along the x-axis. If it involves x^2, the axis is along the y-axis. The sign of the coefficient of x (or y) fixes the direction: y^2 = 4ax opens right; y^2 = -4ax opens left; x^2 = 4ay opens up; x^2 = -4ay opens down.

PART 3 — Extract a and read off all features.
Match the given equation to one of the four standard forms to read off a. For y^2 = 12x: 4a = 12 so a = 3. Focus = (3, 0), directrix x = -3, vertex = (0, 0), latus rectum = 12. For x^2 = 8y: 4a = 8 so a = 2. Focus = (0, 2), directrix y = -2, latus rectum = 8.
keyMoves: Set up PF = PB and square to derive y^2 = 4ax
Read orientation: y^2 term -> axis along x; x^2 term -> axis along y
Use the sign of the coefficient to fix the opening direction, then solve for a
misconceptions: Using y^2 = 4ax for a parabola that actually opens along the y-axis
Getting the opening direction wrong by ignoring the sign of the coefficient

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of a parabola as 4a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of a parabola and show its length is 4a for y^2 = 4ax.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of a parabola is a line segment perpendicular to the axis of the parabola, through the focus and whose end points lie on the parabola
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define the latus rectum.
The latus rectum of a parabola is the chord that passes through the focus and is perpendicular to the axis. Its two endpoints lie on the parabola, and it is the shortest focal chord of the parabola.

PART 2 — Compute its length for y^2 = 4ax.
For y^2 = 4ax, the focus is at (a, 0). Setting x = a in the equation: y^2 = 4a*a = 4a^2, so y = +/-2a. The two endpoints of the latus rectum are (a, 2a) and (a, -2a). The length of the latus rectum is therefore 2a - (-2a) = 4a.

PART 3 — Use it to summarise a parabola completely.
Once you have matched an equation to standard form and found a, you can immediately state: vertex = (0,0), focus = (a, 0), directrix x = -a, and latus rectum = 4a. For y^2 = 12x: a = 3, latus rectum = 12. For x^2 = 8y: a = 2, latus rectum = 8.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the axis
Use AF = FM = 2a and symmetry to get total length 4a
Combine with the standard form to report focus, directrix and latus rectum together
misconceptions: Reporting the latus rectum as 2a (half the chord) instead of 4a
Measuring the latus rectum along the axis rather than perpendicular to it

nodes.title: Define an ellipse by the constant sum of focal distances
nodes.brief: State that an ellipse is the locus of points whose distances from two foci add to a constant, and name its centre, major and minor axes and vertices.
nodes.quote: An *ellipse* is the set of all points in a plane, the sum of whose distances from two fixed points in the plane is a constant.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The locus condition: constant sum of focal distances.
An ellipse is the set of all points P in a plane such that PF1 + PF2 = constant, where F1 and F2 are two fixed points called the foci. This constant sum is always greater than the distance F1F2 between the foci. The midpoint of the segment F1F2 is the centre of the ellipse.

PART 2 — Axes and vertices.
The line segment through the foci is called the major axis; its length is 2a (equal to the constant sum). The segment through the centre perpendicular to the major axis is the minor axis; its length is 2b. The endpoints of the major axis are the vertices of the ellipse.

PART 3 — Why the sum must exceed F1F2.
If PF1 + PF2 were less than F1F2 no point P could exist; if it equalled F1F2 the only 'points' would be on the segment F1F2 itself (degenerate case). For PF1 + PF2 > F1F2 the locus is a proper closed oval curve — the ellipse.
keyMoves: State the locus condition PF1 + PF2 = constant (= 2a)
Name the two fixed points the foci and the midpoint of their segment the centre
Identify major axis, minor axis and the vertices
misconceptions: Using the difference of focal distances (that defines a hyperbola, not an ellipse)
Taking the constant sum to be less than the distance between the foci

nodes.title: Relate the semi-axes and focal distance of an ellipse (a^2 = b^2 + c^2)
nodes.brief: Use the lengths 2a, 2b, 2c (major axis, minor axis, distance between foci) and the relation a^2 = b^2 + c^2.
nodes.quote: We denote the length of the major axis by 2a, the length of the minor axis by 2b and the distance between the foci by 2c.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Name the three lengths.
For an ellipse, let 2a = length of the major axis, 2b = length of the minor axis, and 2c = distance between the foci. So a is the semi-major axis, b the semi-minor axis, and c the semi-focal distance. We have a > b > 0 and a > c >= 0.

PART 2 — Derive a^2 = b^2 + c^2 using two special points.
At a vertex (end of major axis), say V = (a, 0): PF1 + PF2 = (a + c) + (a - c) = 2a. That confirms the constant sum equals 2a. At an end of the minor axis, say B = (0, b): both focal distances equal sqrt(b^2 + c^2). So 2*sqrt(b^2 + c^2) = 2a, giving a^2 = b^2 + c^2.

PART 3 — Rearrangement and use.
Equivalently c^2 = a^2 - b^2, or c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2). Given any two of {a, b, c} the third is determined. For x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1: a = 5, b = 3, c = sqrt(25 - 9) = sqrt(16) = 4.
keyMoves: Set major axis = 2a, minor axis = 2b, distance between foci = 2c
Show the constant sum is 2a using the major-axis endpoint
Derive a^2 = b^2 + c^2 (so c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2)) using the minor-axis endpoint
misconceptions: Writing c^2 = a^2 + b^2 (the hyperbola relation) instead of a^2 = b^2 + c^2
Confusing the semi-axes a, b with the full axis lengths 2a, 2b

nodes.title: Compute the eccentricity of an ellipse as e = c/a
nodes.brief: Define the eccentricity e = c/a of an ellipse and use it to place the foci at distance ae from the centre.
nodes.quote: The eccentricity of an ellipse is the ratio of the distances from the centre of the ellipse to one of the foci and to one of the vertices of the ellipse
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define eccentricity e = c/a.
The eccentricity of an ellipse is e = c/a, where c is the semi-focal distance and a is the semi-major axis. Since 0 < c < a for any proper ellipse, the eccentricity satisfies 0 < e < 1.

PART 2 — What eccentricity measures.
A smaller e (closer to 0) means the foci are close to the centre and the ellipse is nearly circular. A larger e (closer to 1) means the foci are far from the centre and the ellipse is elongated. When e = 0 the two foci coincide at the centre and the ellipse becomes a circle.

PART 3 — Compute from standard form.
For x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1: a = 5, b = 3, c = sqrt(25 - 9) = 4, so e = 4/5. For x^2/16 + y^2/7 = 1: a = 4, b = sqrt(7), c = sqrt(16 - 7) = 3, so e = 3/4. The foci lie at (+-ae, 0) = (+-c, 0).
keyMoves: Compute c from a^2 = b^2 + c^2, then form e = c/a
Check 0 < e < 1 for an ellipse
Locate the foci at distance ae from the centre
misconceptions: Computing e as a/c instead of c/a
Expecting e >= 1 for an ellipse (that holds for a hyperbola)

nodes.title: Use the standard equation x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 of an ellipse
nodes.brief: Apply the standard equation of an ellipse, read which axis is major from the larger denominator, and extract foci, vertices and axis lengths.
nodes.quote: These two equations are known as standard equations of the ellipses.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two standard forms depending on which axis is major.
With the centre at the origin and foci on the x-axis, the standard form is x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 with a > b > 0. With foci on the y-axis, the roles of a and b swap: x^2/b^2 + y^2/a^2 = 1 (a > b). In both cases the major axis lies along the variable with the larger denominator.

PART 2 — Read a, b, c and locate foci and vertices.
From x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (foci on x-axis): vertices are (+-a, 0), co-vertices (0, +-b), foci (+-c, 0) with c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2). From x^2/b^2 + y^2/a^2 = 1 (foci on y-axis): vertices (0, +-a), foci (0, +-c).

PART 3 — Build the equation from given conditions.
If given foci and major-axis length, compute a and c, then b^2 = a^2 - c^2, and write the equation. Example: foci (+-4, 0), major axis 2a = 10 gives a = 5, b^2 = 25 - 16 = 9, equation x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1.
keyMoves: Identify the major axis from the larger denominator (foci lie on it)
Read a and b from the standard form and compute c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2)
Report foci, vertices and axis lengths, or solve for a^2, b^2 from given data
misconceptions: Assuming the major axis is always along the x-axis regardless of which denominator is larger
Putting the foci on the minor axis instead of the major axis

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of an ellipse as 2b^2/a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of an ellipse and show its length is 2b^2/a.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of an ellipse is a line segment perpendicular to the major axis through any of the foci and whose end points lie on the ellipse
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define the latus rectum of an ellipse.
The latus rectum of an ellipse is the chord that passes through a focus and is perpendicular to the major axis, with both endpoints on the ellipse. There are two such chords (one through each focus), and by symmetry they have equal length.

PART 2 — Derive its length as 2b^2/a.
For x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (foci on x-axis), the right focus is at (c, 0). Substitute x = c: c^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1, so y^2 = b^2(1 - c^2/a^2) = b^2*(a^2 - c^2)/a^2 = b^4/a^2 (using b^2 = a^2 - c^2). Thus y = +/-b^2/a. The half-length is b^2/a and the full latus rectum is 2b^2/a.

PART 3 — Apply to examples.
For x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1 (a=5, b=3): latus rectum = 2(9)/5 = 18/5. For x^2/16 + y^2/7 = 1 (a=4, b=sqrt(7)): latus rectum = 2(7)/4 = 7/2.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the major axis
Substitute x = ae into the standard equation to get the half-length b^2/a
Double it for the full length 2b^2/a
misconceptions: Quoting the latus rectum as b^2/a (the half-length) instead of 2b^2/a
Using 2a^2/b by swapping the roles of a and b

nodes.title: Define a hyperbola by the constant difference of focal distances
nodes.brief: State that a hyperbola is the locus of points whose distances from two foci differ by a constant, and name its centre, transverse and conjugate axes and vertices.
nodes.quote: A hyperbola is the set of all points in a plane, the difference of whose distances from two fixed points in the plane is a constant.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The locus condition: constant difference of focal distances.
A hyperbola is the set of all points P in a plane such that |PF1 - PF2| = constant, where F1 and F2 are two fixed points called the foci. The difference (taken as absolute value, or equivalently the farther distance minus the nearer distance) is always positive and equals 2a. This is the counterpart of the ellipse's constant sum.

PART 2 — Axes, centre, and vertices.
The midpoint of the segment F1F2 is the centre. The line through the foci is the transverse axis (length 2a); the points where the curve meets the transverse axis are the vertices. The line through the centre perpendicular to the transverse axis is the conjugate axis. Unlike the ellipse, a hyperbola consists of two separate branches.

PART 3 — Why |PF1 - PF2| must be less than F1F2.
By the triangle inequality |PF1 - PF2| < F1F2, i.e. 2a < 2c. If the difference equalled F1F2 the locus would degenerate. For 0 < 2a < 2c the locus is a genuine two-branched hyperbola.
keyMoves: State the locus condition PF1 - PF2 = constant (= 2a)
Read 'difference' as farther distance minus nearer distance
Identify centre, transverse axis, conjugate axis and the vertices
misconceptions: Using the sum of focal distances (that defines an ellipse, not a hyperbola)
Confusing the transverse axis (through the foci) with the conjugate axis

nodes.title: Relate the axes and focal distance of a hyperbola (b^2 = c^2 - a^2)
nodes.brief: Use 2c (distance between foci), 2a (transverse axis) and the defining quantity b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2), with 2b the conjugate axis.
nodes.quote: Also 2b is the length of the conjugate axis
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Name the three lengths.
For a hyperbola, let 2c = distance between the foci, 2a = length of the transverse axis, and define b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2). Then 2b is called the length of the conjugate axis. Note that c > a for a hyperbola (unlike the ellipse where a > c).

PART 2 — The key relation c^2 = a^2 + b^2.
From the definitions, b^2 = c^2 - a^2, i.e. c^2 = a^2 + b^2. This is the opposite sign pattern from the ellipse (a^2 = b^2 + c^2). Here b is not a semi-axis of the hyperbola curve itself but the half-length of the conjugate axis, which does not intersect the hyperbola.

PART 3 — Contrast with the ellipse.
Ellipse: a > b, a > c, a^2 = b^2 + c^2. Hyperbola: c > a, c^2 = a^2 + b^2. The conjugate axis of a hyperbola and the semi-axes of the auxiliary rectangle (used to draw the asymptotes) have lengths a and b; the asymptotes pass through the corners of this rectangle.
keyMoves: Set distance between foci = 2c, transverse axis = 2a
Define b = sqrt(c^2 - a^2), i.e. c^2 = a^2 + b^2, with 2b the conjugate axis
Note c > a, so c^2 = a^2 + b^2 (the opposite sign-pattern to the ellipse)
misconceptions: Using a^2 = b^2 + c^2 (the ellipse relation) instead of c^2 = a^2 + b^2
Taking the transverse axis to be the conjugate axis

nodes.title: Compute the eccentricity of a hyperbola as e = c/a (>= 1)
nodes.brief: Define the eccentricity e = c/a of a hyperbola and note it is never less than one, with the foci at distance ae from the centre.
nodes.quote: is called the *eccentricity of the hyperbola*. Since
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define eccentricity e = c/a for a hyperbola.
The eccentricity of a hyperbola is e = c/a, the same formula as for an ellipse. However, since for a hyperbola c > a (the foci lie outside the vertices), the eccentricity satisfies e > 1. This is the key numerical distinction: ellipse has e < 1, parabola has e = 1, hyperbola has e > 1.

PART 2 — What large e means geometrically.
A hyperbola with e close to 1 has branches that open narrowly (nearly parabolic). As e increases the asymptotes become more widely spread and the branches open more broadly. The asymptotes of x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 have slopes +/-b/a; a larger b/a ratio corresponds to a larger e.

PART 3 — Compute from standard form.
For x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1: a = 3, b = 4, c = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5, e = 5/3. For x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1: a = 4, b = 3, c = sqrt(16 + 9) = 5, e = 5/4. Note e > 1 in both cases.
keyMoves: Compute c from c^2 = a^2 + b^2, then form e = c/a
Note e >= 1 for a hyperbola (versus e < 1 for an ellipse)
Place the foci at distance ae from the centre
misconceptions: Expecting e < 1 for a hyperbola
Computing c from a^2 = b^2 + c^2 instead of c^2 = a^2 + b^2

nodes.title: Use the standard equation x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 of a hyperbola
nodes.brief: Apply the standard equation of a hyperbola, read the transverse axis from the positive term, and extract foci, vertices and lengths.
nodes.quote: These two equations are known as the standard equations of hyperbolas.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two standard forms depending on orientation.
With centre at the origin and foci on the x-axis, the standard hyperbola equation is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1. The transverse axis is along the x-axis and the vertices are at (+-a, 0). With foci on the y-axis, the equation is y^2/a^2 - x^2/b^2 = 1 with vertices at (0, +-a). The transverse axis always lies along the variable in the positive term.

PART 2 — Read a, b, c and locate key features.
For x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1: vertices (+-a, 0), foci (+-c, 0) with c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2), asymptotes y = +/-(b/a)x. For y^2/a^2 - x^2/b^2 = 1: vertices (0, +-a), foci (0, +-c), asymptotes y = +/-(a/b)x.

PART 3 — Build the equation from given conditions.
If given foci (+-c, 0) and transverse axis 2a, compute b^2 = c^2 - a^2, then write x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1. Example: foci (+-5, 0), transverse axis 6 gives a = 3, b^2 = 25 - 9 = 16, equation x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1.
keyMoves: Identify the transverse axis from the positive term (foci lie on it)
Read a and b from the standard form and compute c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2)
Report foci, vertices and transverse-axis length, or solve for a^2, b^2 from given data
misconceptions: Taking the transverse axis from the larger denominator rather than the positive term
Using c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2) (the ellipse formula) for a hyperbola

nodes.title: Compute the latus rectum of a hyperbola as 2b^2/a
nodes.brief: Define the latus rectum of a hyperbola and use its length 2b^2/a.
nodes.quote: Latus rectum of hyperbola is a line segment perpendicular to the transverse axis through any of the foci and whose end points lie on the hyperbola.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define the latus rectum of a hyperbola.
The latus rectum of a hyperbola is the focal chord perpendicular to the transverse axis, with both endpoints on the hyperbola. There are two such chords (one through each focus), each of the same length 2b^2/a.

PART 2 — Derive its length.
For x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, substitute x = c (the right focus): c^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, so y^2 = b^2(c^2/a^2 - 1) = b^2*(c^2 - a^2)/a^2 = b^4/a^2 (using b^2 = c^2 - a^2). Thus y = +/-b^2/a, and the full latus rectum length is 2b^2/a.

PART 3 — Apply to examples.
For x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1 (a=3, b=4): latus rectum = 2(16)/3 = 32/3. For x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1 (a=4, b=3): latus rectum = 2(9)/4 = 9/2. The formula 2b^2/a is identical in form to the ellipse — only the meaning of b differs.
keyMoves: Define the latus rectum: focal chord perpendicular to the transverse axis
Apply the length formula 2b^2/a
Combine with the standard form to report all of foci, vertices, eccentricity, latus rectum
misconceptions: Using 2a^2/b by swapping a and b
Measuring the latus rectum along the transverse axis instead of perpendicular to it

nodes.title: Derive standard conic equations from their geometric definitions
nodes.brief: Turn the focus/directrix or distance-sum/difference definition of any conic into its standard equation and read off its key features.
nodes.quote: In the following sections, we shall obtain the equations of each of these conic sections in standard form by defining them based on geometric properties.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — One method for all conics: locus definition to standard equation.
Every conic in this chapter is derived by the same three-step method: (1) state the locus definition (a geometric equidistance or sum/difference condition); (2) apply the distance formula to that condition; (3) simplify algebraically to reach the standard equation. The circle uses |CP|=r; the parabola uses PF=PD; the ellipse uses PF1+PF2=2a; the hyperbola uses |PF1-PF2|=2a.

PART 2 — Summary table of standard equations and key features.
Circle: (x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=r^2, centre (h,k), radius r. Parabola: y^2=4ax, focus (a,0), directrix x=-a, latus rectum 4a. Ellipse: x^2/a^2+y^2/b^2=1 (a>b), a^2=b^2+c^2, e=c/a<1, latus rectum 2b^2/a. Hyperbola: x^2/a^2-y^2/b^2=1, c^2=a^2+b^2, e=c/a>1, latus rectum 2b^2/a.

PART 3 — Read features from the equation or build the equation from conditions.
Given an equation, match to the standard form, extract a and b, compute c, then read off foci, vertices, eccentricity and latus rectum. Given conditions (foci, axis length, eccentricity), reverse the process: find a, b (or r), and write the equation. Both directions are tested in JEE.
keyMoves: Start from the conic's locus definition and apply the distance formula
Simplify to the standard equation and identify a, b, c (and the centre)
Read off vertices, foci, axes, eccentricity and latus rectum, or build the equation from given conditions
misconceptions: Treating the four conics as needing four unrelated methods rather than one locus-to-equation procedure
Mixing up the ellipse relation a^2 = b^2 + c^2 with the hyperbola relation c^2 = a^2 + b^2
